fields.brief_block: The fact that counsel was subsequently disbarred does not itself show ineffective assistance in a particular case.
fields.opinion_block: Lastly, the fact that post-trial counsel was subsequently disbarred does not itself show ineffective assistance in Anthony's particular case. [...] Disbarment alone is not sufficient to change the standard for ineffective assistance, and Anthony has not shown that post-trial counsel's disciplinary matters were related to this case or that post-trial counsel performed deficiently.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion directly supports the proposition. In its concluding paragraph (Section V), the Georgia Supreme Court holds that "the fact that post-trial counsel was subsequently disbarred does not itself show ineffective assistance in Anthony's particular case," and explains that disbarment alone does not change the ineffective-assistance standard absent a showing that the disciplinary matters were related to the case or that counsel performed deficiently. The brief's quotation is accurate; its only departures from the opinion are a properly bracketed "[a]" substituted for "Anthony's" and the dropping of "post-trial," neither of which alters the meaning, so the CLOSE quote flag reflects cosmetic rather than substantive variance.

fields.brief_block: Wainwright has not established a reasonable probability that the result of his trial would have been different absent Morris's alleged deficiencies; mere speculation that counsel failed to properly cross-examine a witness is not enough to show prejudice.
fields.opinion_block: Appellant maintains that his trial counsel performed deficiently in failing to properly cross-examine Cassinova Houston . . . . To show prejudice from these alleged deficiencies, however, Appellant was “required to offer more than mere speculation that, absent the counsel's alleged errors, a different result probably would have occurred at trial.”
fields.finding_analysis: Baker v. State directly supports the proposition: in Division 3 (page 815), the court rejected an ineffective-assistance claim premised on counsel's alleged failure to properly cross-examine a witness (Cassinova Houston), holding that to show prejudice the appellant was "required to offer more than mere speculation that, absent the counsel's alleged errors, a different result probably would have occurred at trial," and that he failed to do so. The brief's statement of the prejudice standard and its application to a failure-to-cross-examine theory tracks Baker's holding faithfully.

fields.brief_block: Evidence of mutual combat "may authorize the jury to find the defendant guilty of voluntary manslaughter."
fields.opinion_block: A finding that a defendant was engaged in mutual combat at the time the victim was killed may authorize the jury to find the defendant guilty of voluntary manslaughter and not malice murder.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion states this principle directly in Division 2, holding that "[a] finding that a defendant was engaged in mutual combat at the time the victim was killed may authorize the jury to find the defendant guilty of voluntary manslaughter and not malice murder" (citing Sanders v. State, 283 Ga. 372, 375 (2008)). The brief's quoted language tracks the opinion verbatim; the CLOSE quote-check flag reflects only that the brief stopped the quotation at "voluntary manslaughter" and omitted the trailing "and not malice murder," which does not change the substance. The proposition is accurately supported.

fields.brief_block: A defendant is not entitled to a self-defense charge where the victim initiated a struggle because the defendant threatened him with a firearm.
fields.opinion_block: As evidence that he acted in self-defense, Brunson cites testimony that ... he initiated the fight by rushing at Brunson. Yet, the record shows that Milton moved toward Brunson only because Brunson had threatened Milton with a revolver. ... [T]he trial court did not err in determining that the evidence did not warrant the giving of a self-defense charge.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion directly supports the proposition. In Division 2, Brunson argued he was entitled to a self-defense instruction in part because the victim (Milton) initiated the fight by rushing at him, but the court held no charge was warranted because "the record shows that Milton moved toward Brunson only because Brunson had threatened Milton with a revolver," and accordingly affirmed the trial court's refusal to give a self-defense charge. That is precisely the principle the brief attributes to the case, and the pincite (227-228) is where the discussion appears.

fields.brief_block: Cronic's constructive denial of counsel exception to the general Strickland standard is a narrow one that applies only when there was a breakdown in the adversarial process such that counsel entirely fails to subject the prosecution's case to meaningful adversarial testing.
fields.opinion_block: Appellant suggests, however, that his case comes within the "narrow exception" to Strickland's two-part test for cases in which "there was a breakdown in the adversarial process," such that "counsel entirely fails to subject the prosecution's case to meaningful adversarial testing," allowing prejudice to be presumed.
fields.finding_analysis: Charleston, at 682, frames the Cronic exception in precisely these terms — a "narrow exception" to Strickland's two-part test confined to cases of "a breakdown in the adversarial process" where "counsel entirely fails to subject the prosecution's case to meaningful adversarial testing," and (at 683) labels it the "constructive denial of counsel" exception. The proposition tracks the opinion's own language and holding. The CLOSE quote flag reflects only minor seam/punctuation differences; the quoted phrases appear in the opinion essentially verbatim.

fields.brief_block: The Cronic exception requires an attorney's failure [to] be complete and must occur throughout the proceeding and not merely at specific points.
fields.opinion_block: Likewise, this Court has explained that for the Cronic "constructive denial of counsel" exception to apply, the "attorney's failure must be complete" and must occur throughout the proceeding and not merely at specific points.
fields.finding_analysis: Charleston at 682-683 (quoting Turpin v. Curtis and Bell v. Cone) states the rule almost word-for-word: the "attorney's failure must be complete" and "must occur throughout the proceeding and not merely at specific points." The proposition is accurately supported. The brief's bracketed "[to]" substitutes for the opinion's "must" within the quoted phrase, which accounts for the CLOSE flag, but the substance is faithful to the opinion.

fields.brief_block: Given that co-counsel was present throughout, Morris's cross-examination of a single witness after missing most of her direct examination does not meet the stringent Cronic standard, and Strickland's two-part test remains appropriate.
fields.opinion_block: Appellant's assertion that his "trial counsel failed to act as an advocate on several occasions" does not meet this stringent standard. We therefore evaluate Appellant's ineffective assistance claims under the usual Strickland deficient performance and resulting prejudice test.
fields.finding_analysis: Charleston at 683 holds that an assertion of counsel failing "on several occasions" — i.e., at specific points rather than completely and throughout — "does not meet this stringent standard," and that the court therefore applies the usual Strickland two-part test. The brief invokes exactly that standard and applies it by analogy to its own facts (a partial, specific-point failure), and the quoted phrase "does not meet this stringent standard" appears verbatim. The case-specific facts (Morris, co-counsel present, a single witness) are the brief's own and are not found in Charleston, but the legal proposition the case is cited for is squarely supported.

fields.brief_block: There is no inherent violation of a defendant's constitutional right to counsel when an associate of lead counsel represents the defendant.
fields.opinion_block: Therefore, representation by the associate of the lead attorney did not violate Cox's constitutional right to counsel.
fields.finding_analysis: Division 6 of Cox v. State squarely supports this proposition. After Cox's retained lead attorney became ill mid-trial and an associate took over with the client's on-the-record permission, the court held that "representation by the associate of the lead attorney did not violate Cox's constitutional right to counsel." That holding directly matches the brief's assertion, and the pincite to page 226 correctly locates the start of the relevant division.

fields.brief_block: When a merger error benefits a defendant and the State fails to raise it by cross-appeal, the court generally does not correct the error.
fields.opinion_block: [W]hen a merger error benefits a defendant and the State fails to raise it by cross-appeal, we henceforth will exercise our discretion to correct the error upon our own initiative only in exceptional circumstances.
fields.finding_analysis: Division 4 of the opinion (at page 698) adopts precisely this rule, holding that when a merger error benefits a defendant and the State does not raise it by cross-appeal, the court will correct it sua sponte only in exceptional circumstances, and the court declined to correct the erroneous merger here. The brief's characterization that the court "generally does not correct the error" accurately reflects that holding, since correction is reserved for exceptional cases.

fields.brief_block: To be entitled to a new trial based upon the denial of his motion for continuance, the defendant has the burden to show that he was harmed by that denial.
fields.opinion_block: Furthermore, "to be entitled to a new trial based upon the denial of [a] motion for a continuance, [a defendant has] the burden to show that he was harmed by that denial." (Footnote omitted.) Id.
fields.finding_analysis: Division 3 of Geiger states the rule almost verbatim: the defendant bears the burden to show he was harmed by the denial of a continuance. The pincite to page 651 is accurate, and the brief's paraphrase tracks the opinion's language closely. This is a direct and accurate use of the case.

fields.brief_block: Mere speculation and conjecture that harm occurred is not enough to show harmful error in a continuance denial.
fields.opinion_block: Because of this, Geiger cannot show harm from the trial court's denial of his second motion for a continuance on the basis that he was allegedly improperly denied the opportunity to independently test the shell casing. See Williams v. State, [...] (defendant failed to show prejudice from denial of his motion for a continuance where he presented no evidence at motion for new trial hearing to indicate that his defense would have benefitted [...]).
fields.finding_analysis: Geiger is squarely on the topic of harm from a continuance denial, and its reasoning is consistent with the brief's point: the court found Geiger could not show harm because he never had the shell casing tested and presented no evidence that testing would have been favorable, so his claimed harm rested on conjecture. The opinion's actual standard, however, is framed as a burden to produce evidence of harm ("cannot show harm" where the defendant "presented no evidence"), not as a stated rule that "mere speculation and conjecture" is insufficient. The brief crystallizes the opinion's evidentiary failure into a crisper "speculation and conjecture" standard the opinion does not itself articulate; the holding reasonably extends to the proposition, but the brief slightly overstates by attributing that specific rule to this case.

fields.brief_block: Upon a party's request, the trial court is required to put the jurors in the jury box in groups of 12 for examination of individual jurors because the statute does not provide for the exercise of judicial discretion in this matter.
fields.opinion_block: However, the trial court may exercise discretion concerning the identity of the propounder of voir dire questions and whether those questions are posed to the jury panel en masse, to each panel of 12, or to each juror individually.
fields.finding_analysis:
The quoted sentence does appear in Hammond almost verbatim, but it is recited as the appellant's argument -- the opinion introduces it with "Appellant points out that, upon a party's request, the trial court is required to put the jurors in the jury box in groups of 12 for the examination of individual jurors because the statute does not provide for the exercise of judicial discretion in this matter." It is not the court's holding.

The court's actual analysis cuts the other way. After quoting OCGA § 15-12-131 (which does mandate placing jurors in panels of 12 on request for the examination of individual jurors by counsel), the court held that the trial court "may exercise discretion" over the format of voir dire -- whether questions are posed en masse, to panels of 12, or to each juror individually -- and found no abuse of discretion in having general questions asked of the panel as a whole. The appellant lost on this issue.

The proposition therefore overstates the case: it tracks the statutory language the opinion quotes, and individual jurors were in fact examined in panels of 12, but it presents the appellant's mandatory "no discretion" framing as the holding and omits the court's emphasis on trial-court discretion over voir dire format.

fields.brief_block: To authorize a requested jury instruction, there need only be slight evidence supporting the theory of the charge.
fields.finding_analysis: Hicks v. State states this proposition verbatim at page 262: "To authorize a requested jury instruction, there need only be slight evidence supporting the theory of the charge," citing Davis v. State, and the cited pincite (262) is correct.

fields.brief_block: The evidence presented against Wainwright was sufficient to authorize a rational jury to find beyond a reasonable doubt that he was guilty of the crimes for which he was convicted.
fields.opinion_block: Instead, the relevant question is whether, after viewing the evidence in the light most favorable to the prosecution, any rational trier of fact could have found the essential elements of the crime beyond a reasonable doubt.
fields.finding_analysis: Jackson v. Virginia is the controlling Supreme Court authority for the very standard the brief invokes: it holds that on review of evidentiary sufficiency the question is whether, viewing the evidence in the light most favorable to the prosecution, any rational trier of fact could have found the essential elements of the crime beyond a reasonable doubt. The brief's proposition applies that standard to its own defendant (Wainwright), which is the standard use of this citation; the opinion concerns a different defendant (Jackson, convicted of murdering Mary Cole) only because Jackson is the case that announced the rule, not because the citation resolves to the wrong case. The pincite 318-319 is accurate: the standard is articulated on page 319, and page 318 carries the Winship-based predicate that a conviction cannot stand where no rational trier of fact could find guilt beyond a reasonable doubt. There are no quoted passages to verify.

fields.brief_block: A trial court's error in denying a defendant's request to place the jury in panels of 12 for individual questioning has been held harmless when the evidence of guilt was overwhelming.
fields.opinion_block: In any event, the evidence of guilt in this case was overwhelming, and thus, the error, if any, was harmless.
fields.finding_analysis: Jones v. State directly supports the proposition. In Division 1, the court addressed Jones's claim that the trial court erred in denying his request to seat the jurors in panels of 12 in the jury box under OCGA § 15-12-131, and held in the alternative that "the error, if any, was harmless" given the overwhelming evidence of guilt. The court reinforced this by citing Mathis v. State, where "the court found that the error was harmless in light of the overwhelming evidence of guilt." The pincite to page 723 is accurate, as the harmless-error discussion appears on that page. The brief's phrase "for individual questioning" is a slight mischaracterization of the panels-of-12 procedure (the statutory alternative was questioning jurors en masse), but it does not distort the harmless-error principle for which the case is cited.

fields.brief_block: It would turn the law on its head to allow an armed aggressor, who confronts an unarmed nonthreatening victim, to claim self-defense when the victim is shot during the victim's struggle to disarm the aggressor.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion directly supports this proposition. In rejecting Kilpatrick's argument that the trial court should have charged the jury on self-defense, the court states verbatim at page 904 that "it would turn the law on its head to allow an armed aggressor, who confronts an unarmed nonthreatening victim, to claim self-defense when the victim is shot during the victim's struggle to disarm the aggressor." The quotation is accurate, the pincite to page 904 is correct, and the brief uses the language for exactly the principle the court announced.

fields.brief_block: If an appellant fails to meet his or her burden of proving either prong of the Strickland test, the reviewing court does not have to examine the other prong.
fields.opinion_block: If an appellant fails to meet his or her burden of proving ... either prong of the Strickland test, the reviewing court does not have to examine the other prong.
fields.finding_analysis: Lawrence v. State supports this proposition directly. In Division 2, addressing Lawrence's ineffective-assistance claim, the Georgia Supreme Court states the rule in language identical to the brief's: "If an appellant fails to meet his or her burden of proving either prong of the Strickland test, the reviewing court does not have to examine the other prong" (citing Strickland v. Washington, 466 U.S. 668, at 697). The pincite (286 Ga. 533, 533-534) is also accurate — the sentence spans the page break at *534. The "FABRICATED" quote-check result is a false negative: the verbatim language is present, but a star-pagination marker (*534) is embedded in the middle of the sentence in the source text, which defeated the literal string match.

fields.brief_block: A trial court's error in denying a defendant's request to place the jury in panels of 12 for individual questioning has been held harmless when the evidence of guilt was overwhelming.
fields.opinion_block: In this regard, the State points to the evidence of record as overwhelmingly establishing appellant's guilt. We are compelled to agree with this assessment. [...] Applying the "highly probable" test to the evidence of record in this case, we conclude that the error in the conduct of the voir dire was harmless.
fields.finding_analysis: Mathis directly supports the proposition: the court held that the trial court's denial of the defendant's request to voir dire prospective jurors in the jury box in panels of 12 under OCGA § 15-12-131 was "erroneous as a matter of law," but then found the error harmless under the "highly probable" test because the evidence of guilt — including co-defendant testimony, the undercover officer's testimony, and the appellant's confession — was overwhelming and substantially uncontroverted, and affirmed the judgment.

fields.brief_block: When the evidence shows that a defendant acted with criminal intent, was engaged in a criminal scheme, or was criminally negligent, the affirmative defense of accident is unavailable and a jury charge on accident is not warranted.
fields.finding_analysis: Mills v. State directly supports this proposition. In Division 4 the court applied OCGA § 16-2-2 and held that "[t]o succeed on an affirmative defense of accident, the defendant must show that he acted without criminal intent, was not engaged in a criminal scheme, and was not criminally negligent," then affirmed the trial court's refusal of an accident instruction because Mills's handling of a loaded handgun with his finger on the trigger "shows a degree of culpability that constitutes criminal negligence." The pincite to page 832 falls within this discussion.

fields.brief_block: Palmer's convictions have already been affirmed by this Court.
fields.opinion_block: Finding no error, we affirm. [...] Judgment affirmed. All the Justices concur.
fields.finding_analysis: Palmer v. State directly supports the proposition: the Georgia Supreme Court affirmed Palmer's convictions and sentences, stating "Finding no error, we affirm" in the opening paragraph and "Judgment affirmed" in the conclusion after rejecting his competency, severance, and ineffective-assistance challenges.

fields.brief_block: Without a clear showing of abuse of the trial court's broad discretion, an appellate court will not disturb the trial court's decision to deny a motion for continuance.
fields.finding_analysis: Phoenix v. State directly states this rule in Division 2: "In considering a motion for continuance, the trial court enjoys broad discretion" and "Without a clear showing of abuse of this broad discretion, this Court will not disturb a trial court's decision to deny a motion for continuance." The brief's "an appellate court" accurately renders the opinion's "this Court," and the proposition matches the holding precisely.

fields.brief_block: To be entitled to a new trial based upon the denial of his motion for continuance, the defendant has the burden to show that he was harmed by that denial.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion states the principle almost verbatim: "to be entitled to a new trial based upon the denial of a motion for a continuance, a defendant has the burden to show that he was harmed by that denial." The quoted string appears word-for-word, and the proposition is fully supported.

fields.brief_block: Mere speculation and conjecture that harm occurred is not enough to show harmful error in a continuance denial.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion, applying the harm requirement to the denied continuance, quotes Wynn v. State for the proposition that to show harm a party must "specifically identify what other evidence or witnesses he would have put forth in his defense if his counsel had been given more time to prepare; speculation and conjecture are not enough." This squarely supports the brief's statement that speculation and conjecture about harm are insufficient in a continuance-denial context.

fields.brief_block: A defendant who was the aggressor is not entitled to a jury instruction on justification.
fields.finding_analysis: Powers v. State directly supports this proposition: at page 349 the court states that "a defendant is not entitled to a jury instruction on justification when the evidence is that the supposedly justified party was the aggressor," citing OCGA § 16-3-21 (b) (3) and applying it to deny Appellant's requested justification charge because the shooter was the original aggressor. The quoted language appears verbatim in the opinion, and the pincite (349) is accurate.

fields.brief_block: Error in denying a request to place jurors in panels of 12 will not warrant reversal if the defendant is not harmed by it.
fields.opinion_block: The denial in this instance, however, did not cause the appellant any harm. ... Any harm caused by the judge's refusal to put the jury in the jury box upon request was cured by calling a recess and placing the jurors in the boxes along the sides of the courtroom. We find no reversible error.
fields.finding_analysis: Division 2 of Raven v. State supports this proposition directly: the court held that the trial judge's refusal to place the prospective jurors in the jury box upon request, though error, caused the appellant no harm because it was cured by a recess after which the jurors were placed in the boxes, and the court accordingly found "no reversible error." The brief describes the request as one to place jurors "in panels of 12" while the opinion frames it as a request to place jurors "in the jury box" during voir dire, but this is the same jury-box-placement procedure, and the harmless-error principle the case is cited for is squarely what the opinion holds.

fields.brief_block: The fact that counsel was subsequently disbarred does not itself show ineffective assistance in a particular case.
fields.opinion_block: [C]ounsel's voluntary surrender of his license "does not compel the conclusion that his representation of [Simmons] satisfies the test for ineffective assistance of counsel under Strickland v. Washington." . . . [C]ounsel's professional misconduct in an unrelated matter "does not establish either inadequacy or prejudice to [Simmons] in his criminal case."
fields.finding_analysis: Section 10(a) of Simmons v. State directly supports the proposition: the court holds that trial counsel's subsequent surrender of his license to practice law, along with a pending grievance and professional misconduct in an unrelated matter, does not compel a finding of ineffective assistance under Strickland. The only minor gap is terminological -- the opinion describes counsel's "voluntary surrender of his license" rather than "disbar[ment]" -- but the substance is identical, so the citation fairly supports the point that a lawyer's later loss of license does not itself establish ineffectiveness in the case at hand.

fields.brief_block: To prevail on a claim of ineffective assistance of counsel, a defendant generally must show both that counsel's performance was deficient and that the deficient performance prejudiced the defendant -- a reasonable probability that the result of the trial would have been different absent counsel's deficient performance.
fields.finding_analysis: Strickland is the source of the two-prong test the brief states, and the cited pincite range is on point. Section III (page 687) sets out both components verbatim: "the defendant must show that counsel's performance was deficient" and "the defendant must show that the deficient performance prejudiced the defense," and "[u]nless a defendant makes both showings" relief is unavailable. The Court then defines the prejudice prong (page 694) as requiring "a reasonable probability that, but for counsel's unprofessional errors, the result of the proceeding would have been different," which matches the brief's reasonable-probability formulation. The proposition is accurately supported.

fields.brief_block: Because the evidence shows that Wainwright's aggravated assault of Sanusi by shooting him was part of the same transaction as the armed robbery of Sanusi, the aggravated assault conviction should have been merged.
fields.opinion_block: Because aggravated assault does not require proof of any element that armed robbery does not, convictions for both offenses will merge — but only if the crimes are part of the same "act or transaction."
fields.finding_analysis: Thomas v. State directly states the merger rule the brief relies on. In Division 3, at page 880, the court holds that aggravated assault merges with armed robbery — because aggravated assault requires no element that armed robbery does not — so long as the two offenses are part of the same "act or transaction," and it applies that rule to merge the Krause aggravated-assault conviction (Count 8) with the corresponding armed-robbery count because both arose from the same gunpoint taking. The brief's proposition — that an aggravated assault by shooting that is part of the same transaction as the armed robbery of the same victim should have merged — is an accurate application of that holding, and the pincite to page 880 is correct.

fields.brief_block: The Cronic exception requires an attorney's failure to be complete and must occur throughout the proceeding and not merely at specific points.
fields.opinion_block: The “attorney’s failure must be complete” and must occur throughout the proceeding and not merely at specific points.
fields.finding_analysis: In discussing the narrow Cronic-based presumption of prejudice for constructive denial of counsel, the opinion states verbatim that "[t]he 'attorney's failure must be complete' and must occur throughout the proceeding and not merely at specific points," which directly and accurately supports the brief's proposition. The passage follows United States v. Cronic and explains the standard for the constructive-denial exception, so the brief's attribution of this principle to the Cronic exception is correct.

fields.brief_block: Wainwright argues that prejudice should be presumed because he was effectively denied effective assistance of counsel at a critical stage of his trial, citing the constructive denial of counsel doctrine.
fields.opinion_block: Most obvious, of course, is the complete denial of counsel. The presumption that counsel's assistance is essential requires us to conclude that a trial is unfair if the accused is denied counsel at a critical stage of his trial. Similarly, if counsel entirely fails to subject the prosecution's case to meaningful adversarial testing, then there has been a denial of Sixth Amendment rights that makes the adversary process itself presumptively unreliable.
fields.finding_analysis: Cronic at 659 is precisely where the Court articulates the presumed-prejudice doctrine the brief invokes: it identifies complete denial of counsel, denial at a critical stage of trial, and counsel's entire failure to subject the prosecution's case to meaningful adversarial testing as circumstances in which prejudice is presumed without inquiry into actual performance. The proposition's framing of a presumption arising from constructive denial of counsel at a critical stage tracks the cited page accurately.

fields.brief_block: Cronic's constructive denial of counsel exception to the general Strickland standard is a narrow one that applies only when there was a breakdown in the adversarial process such that counsel entirely fails to subject the prosecution's case to meaningful adversarial testing.
fields.opinion_block: Similarly, if counsel entirely fails to subject the prosecution's case to meaningful adversarial testing, then there has been a denial of Sixth Amendment rights that makes the adversary process itself presumptively unreliable.
fields.finding_analysis: The proposition's key formulation -- that the exception applies only when counsel 'entirely fails to subject the prosecution's case to meaningful adversarial testing' -- is taken verbatim from the opinion at page 659, and the narrowness characterization is borne out by the Court's holding at 662 that 'only when surrounding circumstances justify a presumption of ineffectiveness can a Sixth Amendment claim be sufficient without inquiry into counsel's actual performance at trial.' Cronic and Strickland were companion decisions (footnote 20 cites Strickland 'post'), so describing this as the narrow exception to the general Strickland prejudice requirement is accurate context rather than an overstatement of what the cited pages hold.

fields.brief_block: Reluctance, or fighting to repel an unprovoked attack, is self-defense and should not be confused with mutual combat; there was no evidence to support a finding of mutual combat.
fields.opinion_block: “‘Reluctance, or fighting to repel an unprovoked attack, is self-defense, . . . and should not be confused with mutual combat.’” . . . [T]here was simply no evidence that Brown had the mutual intent to engage in a physical fight with Appellant.
fields.finding_analysis: Watson v. State supports both parts of the proposition. On page 350 the opinion quotes the cited language verbatim — “Reluctance, or fighting to repel an unprovoked attack, is self-defense, . . . and should not be confused with mutual combat” (quoting Berrian v. State) — and it holds there was “simply no evidence that Brown had the mutual intent to engage in a physical fight,” so the trial court did not err in declining a mutual-combat charge. The CLOSE quote flag reflects only the ellipsis formatting (the opinion uses “. . .” rather than “...”); the wording is otherwise an exact match.

fields.brief_block: To prevail on a claim of ineffective assistance of counsel, a defendant generally must show both that counsel's performance was deficient and that the deficient performance prejudiced the defendant.
fields.opinion_block: In order to succeed on this claim, appellant must show that his counsel's performance was professionally deficient and that, but for counsel's unprofessional conduct, there is a reasonable probability the outcome of the proceedings would have been different.
fields.finding_analysis: Wesley v. State states the two-prong ineffective-assistance standard in Division 3, at the cited page 356, requiring a showing that counsel's performance was professionally deficient and that, but for counsel's conduct, there is a reasonable probability the outcome would have been different (citing Strickland v. Washington). That is precisely the deficiency-plus-prejudice rule the brief attributes to the case, so the citation accurately supports the proposition.

fields.brief_block: Morris was disbarred by order of this Court on January 29, 2018, after Wainwright's trial, for issues unrelated to his representation of Wainwright.
fields.opinion_block: Having reviewed the record, we conclude that disbarment is the appropriate sanction in this matter. Accordingly, it is hereby ordered that the name of Ricky W. Morris, Jr., be removed from the rolls of persons authorized to practice law in the State of Georgia. [...] Disbarred. All the Justices concur. [...] Decided January 29, 2018.
fields.finding_analysis: In the Matter of Ricky W. Morris, Jr., 302 Ga. 862, is the Supreme Court of Georgia's per curiam order disbarring Morris, decided January 29, 2018, which supports the proposition on every element. The Court ordered that Morris's name "be removed from the rolls of persons authorized to practice law in the State of Georgia" and the order is dated "Decided January 29, 2018," confirming both the disbarment and the date. The disbarment rested on eleven underlying grievances arising from Morris's handling of various criminal clients (SDB Docket Nos. 6935-6944, 6982), none of which references Wainwright, so the opinion also supports that the discipline was for matters unrelated to Morris's representation of Wainwright. The brief's contextual note that this occurred "after Wainwright's trial" is not addressed by the opinion (which never mentions Wainwright), but that timing detail is framing the citation rather than the substantive point the case is cited to establish.
